body_txt: Samuel Taylor Coleridge , the poet and author, called drama "that willing suspension of disbelief for the moment, which constitutes poetic faith ..." Any creative endeavor—and certainly any fictional story—is only successful to the extent that the audience can willingly agree to just "play along" with the fictional events as they watch, hear or read about them. It's part of an unspoken contract: the writer presents the audience with a good story, in exchange for the audience knowingly accepting that the events within that story will differ from how things work in Real Life (e.g., the fictional universe will have natural and physical laws that differ from the real world that 'we' inhabit). Simply put, a fictional story does not have to be "realistic," but it must not do something which viewers have not been conditioned to accept . Therefore, something which is impossible in Real Life has to be made the norm in your fictional setting in order for viewers to accept it. Audiences are generally willing to go along with creative explanations for fictional concepts like magic or sci-fi gadgetry so long as they feel plausible within the established confines of your story —hence why they don't criticize your faster-than-light wormhole travel system or wonder how a shrinking potion doesn't violate the laws of matter conservation . By contrast, if the author goes outside of the very limits which they have already established for their fictional world (e.g., if a character behaves in a way which does not align with their established personality or motivations), then viewers may feel pushed beyond the limits of what they are prepared to accept, and the work consequently fails in the eyes of that audience. This applies even to the more fantastical genres—in Speculative Fiction , a viewer's suspension of disbelief can still be broken if a work fails to abide by its own rules (although that requirement can sometimes be relieved ) or otherwise expects viewers to put up with too many events that feel contrived. A common way of putting this is: "You can ask an audience to believe the impossible, but not the improbable." For example, people will accept that a wizard can use magic spells to resolve problems ( so long as the explanation for the spell fits the situation ), or that a spaceship has the technology to render itself invisible without also rendering its own sensors blind. But the audience will not accept it if, say, a ferocious carnivore just happens to have a heart attack and die right before it would have eaten the main character, or if a character who has no experience with magic is able to conveniently resolve the story's conflict by accidentally casting a spell that not even the most experienced wizards know how to cast. Without some prior detail or plot point to justify such contrivances (or one of the Rules listed below coming into play), the audience is going to cry foul, and their suspended disbelief is now at the forefront of their mind. Simply put: all stories are contrived by their very nature ( since the writer has to move characters from plot point A to plot points B, C, D, and so on ), but stories can only retain viewers' suspension of disbelief insofar as the contrivances feel justified in-universe . Stories break viewers' suspension of disbelief when the contrivances start to feel forced onto the story by the writer . Boiled down even further: the audience wants to be immersed in your story's world and empathizing with the characters; they don't want to be thinking about you—the writer . Of course, different people will have different thresholds for what they're willing to accept in a work, and what may break one person's willing suspension of disbelief may not necessarily have the same effect on another. With that, there are two major factors that decide how much the audience is willing to suspend their disbelief. One is the very premise of the work that lets the audience know what to expect from the work. The other is the introduction that sets the tone for the work and establishes the rules on how the world works. In more grounded works, the audience's suspension of disbelief can break much more easily. In the other end, if the work establishes itself to be very wacky and surrealist, then the audience may not even question the unfolding events and even the character's motives. Most action movies push this trope almost to the breaking point; for the sake of action, the heroes can do virtually anything , given enough Phlebotinum . As always, the various Rules override nearly all other considerations. When the audience's disbelief, which was suspended during the show, gets reinstated some time afterward, what you get is Fridge Logic . When the means of "fixing" the Plot Hole just end up making it worse and/or opening up a new one, that's a phenomenon known as the Voodoo Shark . This trope dates back at least as far as William Shakespeare and Henry V , in which the narrator directly asks the audience to "on your imaginary forces work" in order to accept a few actors running around a stage as kings and princes and the battle of Agincourt. The MST3K Mantra is an exhortation to reinstate your Willing Suspension of Disbelief even if it's been broken, because "it's just a show". Similarly, Bellisario's Maxim calls the audience to reinstate their WSOD by ignoring whatever Plot Holes or other inconsistencies broke it, or that those things really aren't as important as the audience member(s) may think. Incidentally this is one of the more controversial elements of, believe it or not, Professional Wrestling , and is heavily tied to Kayfabe . When a true story is so bizarre and / or over-the-top that only its veracity enables this, that's Reality Is Unrealistic .
